doom.driver: Trump postpones military strikes on Iranian power plants. This is a breaking news story.
doom.blurb: FIVE DAYS TO TALK THE WORLD OFF THE LEDGE
doom.summary: Trump blinked — or pivoted, depending on your spin — postponing the threatened strikes on Iranian power plants after 48 hours of markets in freefall and the IEA warning of the worst energy crisis in decades. The Strait of Hormuz is still not open, 40+ Middle East energy assets are 'severely damaged,' and Iran is now threatening Gulf power plants AND US Treasury buyers, so 'de-escalation' is doing a lot of heavy lifting here. Call it a stay of execution: the catastrophe dial turns slightly left, but the finger is still on the button.

factors.name: Geopolitics
factors.detail: War enters week four with Trump postponing — not canceling — strikes; Iran threatening Gulf power plants and US Treasury buyers while Iraq is being pulled into the conflict.

factors.name: Governance
factors.detail: DHS shutdown leaves TSA unpaid while ICE deploys to airports, and Trump's threats against broadcasters for 'non-public interest' Iran coverage raise free speech red flags.

factors.name: AI Capability
factors.detail: Musk's Terafab chip plant announcement is a long-horizon compute build, not an immediate capability escalation — background noise today.

factors.name: Alignment
factors.detail: Wired's 'Gods of AI Warfare' feature on Project Maven's true-believer conversion highlights deepening military AI integration with minimal oversight scrutiny.

factors.name: Wild Card
factors.detail: Iranian missiles injuring 180 near Israel's nuclear research site and claims of a downed US F-35 introduce unpredictable escalation vectors that neither side may fully control.

doom.dalle_prompt: Pixel art, 16-bit SNES style, silhouette composition — the Strait of Hormuz at pre-dawn, the narrow sea lane stretching into a horizon consumed by deep crimson and smoldering charcoal light, the rocky brown coastline of Iran a jagged black mass along the top edge, the Musandam Peninsula jutting like a dark fist from below, the water between them an oil-black mirror streaked with blood-red reflections — strong backlight from a distant industrial glow beyond the horizon, the source unseen but volcanic in intensity, casting everything forward into pure silhouette — in the foreground a massive aircraft carrier superstructure rises as a flat black cutout against the burning sky, its island tower and radar arrays rendered as stark geometric shapes, F/A-18 silhouettes spotted motionless on the angled flight deck — to the left the enormous low hull of a fully loaded VLCC oil tanker sits heavy in the water, its rust-streaked mass a black rectangle bleeding into the dark sea — on the far Iranian shoreline truck-mounted coastal missile launchers appear as tiny angular silhouettes frozen in place, barrels aimed outward but still — a pale dome shape, the Bushehr power plant, glows faintly orange on the horizon, backlit and ominous, its cooling infrastructure a crown of dark pylons and cables — the entire scene saturated in dark crimson and charcoal, firelight color bleeding across every surface, no movement, suspended tension, no text, no antialiasing, SNES 16-bit dithering throughout.

dawn.driver: Scientists discover Alzheimer's hidden "death switch" in the brain. Scientists have uncovered a hidden "death switch" in the brain that may be driving Alzheimer's disease—and even found a way to turn it off in mice.
dawn.blurb: SCIENTISTS FIND THE OFF SWITCH FOR ALZHEIMER'S
dawn.summary: Even as the world's attention is consumed by war and energy crisis, researchers have quietly identified the toxic protein pairing that may drive Alzheimer's disease — and demonstrated they can switch it off in mice. This is the kind of incremental, methodical breakthrough that rarely makes front pages but reshapes the future of millions of families. The path from mouse model to human treatment is long and hard, but today, a door opened that wasn't open yesterday.

factors.name: Science
factors.detail: Identifying and experimentally disabling Alzheimer's 'death switch' proteins in mice represents a genuinely novel mechanistic breakthrough in one of medicine's most stubborn puzzles.

factors.name: Cooperation
factors.detail: Trump's five-day postponement of strikes on Iranian energy infrastructure opens a narrow but real window for diplomatic movement after weeks of escalation.

factors.name: Human Spirit
factors.detail: A scientist from Zambia running a lab in South Africa is being hailed for 'extraordinary' drug discovery work, embodying the global democratization of scientific excellence.

factors.name: Environment
factors.detail: New research confirms beavers are transforming streams into carbon-sequestering wetlands, offering a low-cost, nature-driven tool in the climate fight.

factors.name: Wild Card
factors.detail: Australian scientists have demonstrated a working quantum battery prototype capable of ultra-fast 'super absorption' charging, a potential leap for energy storage technology.

dawn.dalle_prompt: Pixel art, 16-bit SNES style, no text, no antialiasing. Night scene inside a darkened Heidelberg neuroscience laboratory, deep shadows consuming the high-ceilinged room, all light radiating from artificial sources — a fluorescence microscope's blue-green eyepiece glows like a lantern in the dimness, large wall monitors casting warm gold and emerald light across adjacent benches crowded with micropipettes and vials. The dominant light source is a towering screen displaying an illuminated neuron diagram in radiant emerald green, its branching dendrites and synapse gaps crackling with tiny pixel sparks of gold, the NMDAR/TRPM4 death complex shown as two dark jagged shapes on the neuron's surface being blocked by a gleaming golden key-shaped molecule. Inside the neuron's body, small bean-shaped mitochondria glow like warm amber lanterns, staying lit, intact. A silver-haired professor stands silhouetted against the monitor, arm raised gesturing toward the protein interaction map, his outline edged in gold from the screen's warmth. A second researcher leans into the fluorescence microscope, face bathed in cold blue-green light from the eyepiece, a moment of silent revelation. In the deep background, dark lab benches recede into shadow, broken only by the soft underglow of lightboxes beneath slides, rich emerald and saturated gold dominating every source of light in the otherwise swallowing darkness.
